title: Difficulty-Aware Unlearning: Closing the Per-Sample Privacy Gap in Machine Unlearning
description: We propose Difficulty-Aware Unlearning (DAU), an adaptive defense that equalizes privacy protection across sample difficulty strata in machine unlearning. Building on the established finding that aggregate MIA-based evaluations mask per-sample privacy failures (Hayes et al., 2025), we bridge Watson et al.'s difficulty calibration framework into the unlearning pipeline: using reference-model difficulty scores to both stratify evaluation (via Worst-Quintile AUC and Difficulty Gap metrics) and drive a continuous per-sample reweighting defense that amplifies unlearning effort on the most vulnerable (hardest) samples.
motivation: Recent work has conclusively shown that aggregate MIA metrics create a false sense of privacy in unlearning evaluation (Hayes et al., SaTML 2025; Rizwan et al., 2024; Zhao et al., NeurIPS 2024). However, while the diagnostic problem is well-understood, the defense problem remains open: no existing method provides a principled, algorithm-agnostic mechanism to equalize privacy protection across difficulty strata. DAU fills this gap by using the same difficulty signal that exposes the problem to also drive the fix.
proposed_approach: Two contributions: (1) Stratified Evaluation — use reference-model difficulty calibration (Watson et al., 2022) to partition forget sets into quintiles and evaluate per-stratum MIA metrics (WQ-AUC, Difficulty Gap), providing a computationally cheaper alternative to per-example U-MIAs (Hayes et al., 2025); (2) Difficulty-Aware Unlearning (DAU) — a wrapper for any gradient-based unlearning method that scales the unlearning loss by per-sample difficulty weights w(x) = 1 + α·(d(x) - d_mean)/d_std, amplifying the unlearning signal for hard (vulnerable) samples. DAU provides continuous per-sample reweighting, complementing Zhao et al.'s (2024) discrete partition-dispatch approach (RUM).

related_work.title: Inexact Unlearning Needs More Careful Evaluations to Avoid a False Sense of Privacy
related_work.relationship: Closest prior work on evaluation. Shows per-example U-MIAs reveal failures masked by aggregate metrics. We differ by: (1) using difficulty calibration as a cheaper stratification mechanism, (2) proposing a defense (DAU), (3) introducing WQ-AUC and DG metrics. We include their per-example U-MIA as an evaluation baseline.

related_work.title: What Makes Unlearning Hard and What to Do About It
related_work.relationship: Studies forget set characteristics affecting difficulty; proposes RUM (partition-dispatch meta-algorithm). DAU differs by providing continuous per-sample reweighting rather than discrete partitioning, and directly targets MIA-detectable privacy gaps.

related_work.title: Towards Understanding the Feasibility of Machine Unlearning
related_work.relationship: Identifies four instance-level factors making unlearning difficult. Our reference-model difficulty scores capture a complementary signal specifically designed to connect to MIA vulnerability.

related_work.title: Toward Understanding Unlearning Difficulty: A Mechanistic Perspective and Circuit-Guided Difficulty Metric
related_work.relationship: Proposes circuit-level difficulty metric (CUD). Provides complementary mechanistic grounding; CUD scores could replace our reference-model scores in future work.

related_work.title: On the Importance of Difficulty Calibration in Membership Inference Attacks
related_work.relationship: Established difficulty calibration for MIA. We bridge this calibration framework into the unlearning evaluation and defense pipeline — a connection not previously made.

related_work.title: Membership Inference Attacks From First Principles
related_work.relationship: State-of-the-art MIA (LiRA) using reference models. We use similar reference model infrastructure for difficulty estimation.

related_work.title: Towards Unbounded Machine Unlearning
related_work.relationship: Proposed SCRUB, a leading unlearning algorithm. We evaluate SCRUB under our framework and propose SCRUB-DAU.

related_work.title: When Machine Unlearning Jeopardizes Privacy
related_work.authors: Chen, Zhang, Wang, Backes, Humbert, Zhang
related_work.relationship: First to show unlearning can increase privacy risk. We analyze how this risk varies with sample difficulty.

related_work.title: Rectifying Privacy and Efficacy Measurements in Machine Unlearning: A New Inference Attack Perspective
related_work.authors: Naderloui, Yan, Wang, Fu, Wang, Liu, Hong
related_work.relationship: Proposed per-sample targeted attacks (RULI) for unlearning evaluation. We differ by using difficulty calibration for stratification and proposing a defense.

related_work.title: Leveraging Per-Instance Privacy for Machine Unlearning
related_work.relationship: Used per-instance DP bounds for unlearning. DAU is algorithm-agnostic and does not require DP machinery.

related_work.title: Is Difficulty Calibration All We Need? Towards More Practical Membership Inference Attacks
related_work.authors: He, Li, Wang, Yang, Wang, Hu, Zhao
related_work.relationship: Identified limitations of difficulty calibration for MIA and proposed RAPID. Our work applies calibration to evaluation rather than attack.

related_work.title: Statistical MIA: Rethinking Membership Inference Attack for Reliable Unlearning Auditing
related_work.relationship: Proposed training-free statistical auditing. Complementary — SMIA could be used as the per-stratum audit in our framework.

related_work.title: Machine Unlearning
related_work.relationship: Foundational exact unlearning work (SISA). We focus on approximate unlearning evaluation and defense.

hypothesis: Current machine unlearning methods exhibit difficulty-dependent privacy gaps: they provide weaker privacy protection for hard (atypical) samples than for easy (typical) samples. Standard aggregate evaluation metrics overestimate unlearning quality by masking these per-stratum failures. A difficulty-calibrated evaluation will quantify these gaps, and Difficulty-Aware Unlearning (DAU) can close them — providing uniform privacy protection across difficulty strata with minimal utility loss.
success_criteria: Worst-Quintile MIA-AUC is significantly higher (>0.05 absolute) than aggregate MIA-AUC for at least 3/5 unlearning methods across datasets
Difficulty Gap (DG) is statistically significant (p < 0.05) for all approximate unlearning methods
DAU reduces Worst-Quintile AUC compared to both standard unlearning and RUM (Zhao et al., 2024) with <1% retain accuracy degradation
Results are consistent across CIFAR-10, CIFAR-100, and Purchase-100 datasets

computational_requirements.key_costs: Training reference models (8 shadow models), training base models (3 seeds x 3 datasets), running 5 unlearning algorithms per config, MIA evaluation, per-example U-MIA baseline
